hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium; dominant MUFA (oleic acid ~78%) supports endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Hazelnut oil contains 0 mg sodium per 100 g and is rich in oleic acid (MUFA ~78%), which supports vascular endothelial function; limit to 1–2 teaspoons per serving due to high caloric density (884 kcal/100 g) and use as a finishing or dressing oil rather than a high-heat cooking fat.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high MUFA content improves insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hazelnut oil contains 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, producing no glycaemic impact; its dominant MUFA profile (oleic acid ~78%) is consistent with ADA dietary fat recommendations for improving insulin sensitivity, but portion control is essential given the high caloric density of 884 kcal per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High MUFA (oleic acid ~78%), vitamin E (47.2 mg/100 g), low saturated fat (7.4 g/100 g), zero trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hazelnut oil's dominant oleic acid content (~78% MUFA) and high vitamin E (47.2 mg/100 g) support LDL reduction and antioxidant protection of lipoproteins, consistent with AHA heart-healthy fat recommendations; saturated fat is 7.4 g/100 g and trans fat is 0 g, making it a cardioprotective choice when used in small quantities (1 tablespoon per serving) as a finishing or dressing oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA/PUFA profile; high vitamin E; zero trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hazelnut oil qualifies for the cold-pressed plant oil MUFA/PUFA carve-out under EULAR dietary recommendations: dominant oleic acid (~78%), vitamin E (47.2 mg/100 g), and zero trans fat confer anti-inflammatory properties; use cold-pressed or virgin hazelnut oil only and store in a dark, cool place to preserve the vitamin E and polyphenol content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hazelnut oil is a golden, clear edible oil cold-pressed or expeller-pressed from the kernels of hazelnuts (Corylus avellana), a tree nut cultivated primarily in Turkey, Italy, and France. The oil has a rich, distinctly nutty aroma and a smooth, delicate flavour that intensifies when used as a finishing oil or salad dressing; it is not recommended for high-heat frying as elevated temperatures degrade its delicate flavour compounds and reduce its vitamin E content. The oil is composed almost entirely of fat (100 g per 100 g), with an energy density of 884 kcal per 100 g, making it one of the most calorie-dense foods available. Its fatty acid profile is dominated by monounsaturated fat in the form of oleic acid (approximately 78 g per 100 g), with polyunsaturated fat at approximately 10.2 g and saturated fat at approximately 7.4 g per 100 g; trans fat is absent. Hazelnut oil is an exceptional dietary source of vitamin E as alpha-tocopherol (approximately 47.2 mg per 100 g), providing potent antioxidant protection against lipid peroxidation. It contains zero carbohydrate, zero sugar, zero protein, and zero sodium, making it naturally suited to ketogenic, low-carbohydrate, and low-sodium dietary patterns. Clinically, the dominant MUFA profile supports LDL cholesterol reduction, vascular endothelial function, and insulin sensitivity, consistent with ADA, AHA, and EULAR dietary fat recommendations for type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis management respectively. Hazelnut oil is a major tree-nut allergen and must be avoided by individuals with hazelnut or tree-nut allergy. It is vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher. Cold-pressed or virgin hazelnut oil retains higher polyphenol and vitamin E content than refined versions and should be stored in a dark, cool place to prevent oxidative rancidity.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171028), an exact match for hazelnut oil. Core macros (energy 884 kcal, fat 100 g, protein 0 g, carbohydrate 0 g) are consistent with the Singapore HPB input data. Fatty acid breakdown (saturated 7.4 g, MUFA 78.0 g, PUFA 10.2 g) and vitamin E (47.2 mg) are from the same USDA entry. No estimation or analogy was required for core values.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is naturally zero — hazelnut oil is a pure refined plant oil containing no protein by formulation, confirmed by USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171028. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — pure plant oil contains no carbohydrate, confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — oil contains no fibre. sugar_g is naturally zero — oil contains no sugar. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero (plant-derived oil, no animal source). vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. water_g is confirmed zero — anhydrous oil. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg are confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 171028. Negative sentinel (-1) fields documentation: vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1 because USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171028) does not report values for these micronutrients for hazelnut oil; these B-vitamins and trace minerals are not expected to be nutritionally significant in a pure refined plant oil and no approved source provides confirmed values. Glycemic index is not applicable: hazelnut oil contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g; GI is not applicable for pure fat foods per the critical q4 rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods; glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) and gi_category is set to not_applicable. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301737 and FoodEx2 code A00LR are used as defensible anchors; SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note as no confirmed code was identified. Allergen: hazelnut oil is a major tree-nut allergen; individuals with hazelnut or tree-nut allergy must avoid this product. Storage: cold-pressed or virgin hazelnut oil should be stored in a dark, cool place to prevent oxidation of the MUFA fraction and degradation of vitamin E.
